title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 1]
1.  Mathematical Equations:
    *   The text mentions the Zeldovich mechanism for NOx formation. Although the specific equations for this mechanism are not provided on this page, it is a well-known set of equations which can be represented in generic format via:
        *   N2 + O <=> NO + N
        *   O2 + N <=> NO + O
        Typically, these equations are rate-limiting and can be described as:
        *    d[NO]/dt = k1[N2][O] - k-1[NO][N] + k2[O2][N] - k-2[NO][O]
        where the k terms are rate constants.

2.  Graphs and Charts:
    *   There are no graphs or charts present on this page excerpt.

3.  Tables:
    *   There are no tables on this page

4.  Diagrams or Schematics:
    *   There are no diagrams or schematics on this page

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 3]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    The document DOES NOT contain any mathematical equations. Only dimensions are provided.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    The document DOES NOT contain any graphs or charts.

3.  **Tables:**

    The document DOES NOT contain any tables.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 1(a): Schematic diagram of a dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle.** This diagram illustrates the internal components and flow patterns within the nozzle. Key components include:
        *   Venturi tube: A converging-diverging section that accelerates the flow.
        *   Pressure atomization nozzle: Responsible for injecting and atomizing the fuel (kerosene).
        *   Inner swirl blades: Induce swirling motion in the inner air stream.
        *   Outer swirl blades: Induce swirling motion in the outer air stream, counter-rotating relative to the inner swirl.
        *   Turbulent shear layer: Region of intense mixing between the inner and outer air streams.
        *   Central recirculation zone: A region of recirculating flow established downstream of the nozzle, crucial for flame stabilization.
    *   **Figure 1(b): Actual image of the dual-stage counter-rotating nozzle and a 3D-printed metal swirler.** This photograph provides a visual representation of the physical hardware.
    *   **Figure 2(a): Key parameters of the nozzle.** This diagram presents specific dimensions of the nozzle components:
        *   Venturi tube (angle and height)
        *   Pressure atomization nozzle height
        *   Height and position of Kerosene injector
        *   Distances between inner and outer swirl blades.
        *   Illustrates the direction of inner and outer swirl air
    *   **Figure 2(b): Key parameters of the confined space.** This diagram shows the geometry of the confined combustion chamber.
        *   The chamber is a rectangular section.
        *   The length of the chamber along the x-axis is 300 mm.
        *   The height of the chamber along the y-axis is 60 mm.
        *   The 'origin' is shown where the nozzle attaches to the x-axis.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 5]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   Equation (4): partial(rhog)/partial(t) + partial(rhog * ui) / partial(xi) = Sm
*   Equation (5): partial(rhog * ui)/partial(t) + partial (rhog * ui * uj) /partial(xj) = -partial(p)/partial(xi) + rhog * g_i + Fs_i
*   Equation (6): partial(rhog * Eg) /partial(t) + partial(uj*(rhog * Eg + p)) /partial(xj) = partial(k_eff * partial(T) / partial(xj) + (T_ij * ui)) /partial(xi) + Sh

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 3 (a):** Shows a schematic of the experimental setup. The setup includes:
    *   *Oil tank*, *volume flow meter*, *oil filter*, *gear pump*, *dual-stage counter rotating nozzle* for fuel side.
    *   *Air compressor*, *Dryer*, *Air tank*, *Pressure reducing valve*, *Mass flow controllers* for air side.
    *   a combustor of *300 mm* long with the flame in the center, as well as an *exhaust fan* and *flue gas analyzer*.
*   **Figure 3 (b):** Provides photographs of components of the experimental setup, showing:
    *   *Volume flowmeter*
    *   *Flue gas analyzer*
    *   *Mass flow controller*
    *   *PIV experiment*
    *   *Flue gas collection rake*
    *   *Confined space (Quartz glass)*
    *   *Combustion experiment*
*   **Figure 4:** Presents a flow diagram of the Euler-Lagrange method for spray combustion simulation.
    *   *Eulerian method*: includes equations for momentum, continuity, and energy. *Models*: Realizable k-epsilon, Component transport, Flamelet Generated Manifold, Discrete Ordinates (DO) Method.
    *   *Lagrangian method*: Includes equations for momentum and energy. *Models*: Atomization, Dynamic-drag, Wave breakup, Dispersion, Stochastic collision, Coalescence, Vaporization.
    *   *Mass exchange*: connects Momentum exchange and Momentum equation.
    *   *Momentum exchange*: connects Momentum equations by both methods.
    *   *Heat exchange*: connects Model parts of two methods.
*   **Figure 5:** A schematic of boundary conditions for the combustion simulation domain on an x-y coordinate plane.
    *   Indicates the location of the *kerosene spray source (DPM source)*, *inner swirl air inlet (mass flow inlet)*, *outer swirl air inlet (mass flow inlet)*, and *flue gas outlet (pressure outlet)*.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   **Table 2:** Lists measurement instruments and uncertainties.
        *   *Air flow rate*: *Mass flow controller*; Uncertainty: ±1%; Range: 0-300 L.min^-1
        *   *Kerosene flow rate*: *WT02A micro elliptical gear flowmeter*; Uncertainty: ±0.5%; Range: 0.3-20 L.h-1
        *   *Velocity*: *LAVISION 1DIC particle image velocimetry system*; Uncertainty: ±1%; Range: 0-20 m.s^-1
        *   *Volumetric fraction of O2*: *Testo 340 flue gas analyzer*; Uncertainty: ±0.2%; Range: 0-25%

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 8]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   Equation (25): av = (Vs * integral_0_to_60mm(S dy)) / Vc = (Vs * integral_0_to_60mm(S dy)) / (60^3 mm^3)

        where av is dimensionless CTRZ volume, Vs is the volume of CTRZ from 0 to 60mm, y is axial distance, S is the cross-sectional area of the CTRZ at the axial distance y, and Vc is the volume of the confined space (60 x 60 x 60 mm^3).

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 7:** Comparison of results under different mesh.
        *   X-axis: Mesh size (Mesh-1, Mesh-2, Mesh-3, Mesh-4) with corresponding cell counts in parentheses (5.7x10^5 - 1.5x10^7)
        *   Y-axis (left): NOx Emission (ppm @ 15% O2)
        *   Y-axis (right): Velocity-y (m/s), for y = 20 mm and y = 40 mm
        *   CO Emission (ppm @ 15% O2): plotted versus mesh size
        *   Trends: CO emission increases with mesh refinement while NO emission decreases slighlt with mesh refinement. Velocity-y values seem to converge as mesh gets refined (Mesh-3 and Mesh-4 give approx. equal velocity curves).

    *   **Figure 8(a):** Comparison of velocity-y between experiment and simulation, at y = 5 mm and y = 30 mm.
        *   X-axis: x (mm)
        *   Y-axis: Velocity-y (m/s)
        *   Four curves are plotted: CFD (y = 5 mm), Exp. (y = 5 mm), CFD (y = 30 mm), and Exp. (y = 30 mm)
        *   Trends: CFD simulates the experimental data relatively well. CFD (y=5mm) and Exp(y=5mm) match very closely, while CFD (y = 30 mm) and Exp(y=30mm) show some deviations, particularly in peak velocity.

   *   **Figure 8(b):** Comparison of velocity-y between experiment and simulation, at x = 0 mm.
        *   X-axis: y (mm)
        *   Y-axis: Velocity-y (m/s)
        *   Two curves are plotted: CFD (x = 0 mm) and Exp. (x = 0 mm)
        *   Trends: CFD predicts the experimental data reasonably well. Magnitude of the reversed flow region (negative velocity) is slightly over predicted.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   **Table 3:** Table of experimental and numerical simulation results.
        *   Columns: "Type", "Exp.", "CFD"
        *   Rows:
            *   NOx emission (ppm @ 15% O2): Exp. = 15.3 +/- 2.1, CFD = 17.38
            *   CO emission (ppm @ 15% O2): Exp. = 5.47 +/- 1.3, CFD = 4.73
            *   Combustion efficiency (%): Exp. = 99.99, CFD = 99.99
        *   Summary: NOx emissions are slightly over predicted. CO emissions are slightly under predicted. Simulations match

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 9]
1.  **Equations:**

    *   Equation (26): eta_q = q_m,s / (q_m,a + q_m,f) = integral(P_mix * vy * |dS_max|) / (q_m,a + q_m,f)

        *   Where:
            *   eta_q = recirculation rate
            *   q_m,s = recirculation mass flow rate at the maximum cross-section on the y axis
            *   q_m,a = total air mass flow rate
            *   q_m,f = total fuel mass flow rate
            *   S_max = the area of the maximum cross-section of the CTRZ on the y axis,
            *   P_mix = fluid density,
            *    vy = axial velocity,
            *    dSmax is presumably differential area element over S_max.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 9:** Schematic of the velocity value extraction locations.

        *   Shows a schematic diagram of the nozzle and confined space section with the origin indicated.  Two data sampling locations are defined:
            *   Location 1:  x ranges from 0 to 30 mm, y = 20 mm, z = 0 mm.
            *   Location 2: x = 0 mm, y ranges from 0 to 250 mm, z = 0 mm.
        *   The schematic sets the coordinate system with x-axis, y-axis

    *   **Figure 10:** 3D view of the iso-surface (Velocity-y = 0 m-s^-1).

        *   Shows a 3D representation of the flow field in the combustor, with an iso-surface representing the location where the y-component of velocity is 0 m/s.
        *   The color scale indicates the magnitude of the y-velocity.
        *   Labels identify the Corner Recirculation Zone (CRZ) and the Central Toroidal Recirculation Zone (CTRZ).
        *   The legend indicates the velocity range of the data, from -6 to 18-20 m/s.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   There are no tables in this excerpt.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 9**  (as described above). Shows the physical setup and sampling locations.
    *   **Figure 10:** Provides a visual representation of the key flow features (CRZ and CTRZ) and their relative positions within the combustor geometry, based on the iso-surface of zero axial velocity. Also provides a color scaled vector field of velocity.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 10]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    The text mentions the following variables:

    *   *a*: inner swirl blade angle
    *   *av*: dimensionless CTRZ volume (note the OCR may have misread the subscript as 'v', it is probably a_v)
    *   *(eta)_q*: recirculation rate

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    The figure (Fig. 11) shows a series of diagrams illustrating the morphology of the Central Toroidal Recirculation Zone (CTRZ) within a gas turbine combustor for varying inner swirl blade angles.

    *   **X-axis:** Not explicitly labeled, but represents the spatial dimension in the axial direction of the combustor.
    *   **Y-axis:** There are two y-axes displayed on each subfigure which represent the degrees in the swirl angle varying from -20 to 20.
    *   **Data Representation:** The CTRZ is visualized using an iso-surface (velocity-y = 0 m/s). This surface represents the regions of zero vertical velocity, implying flow reversal and recirculation. The diagrams are arranged in rows, with the inner swirl blade angle (*a*) increasing from 30° to 51°. Each row contains two diagrams: one labeled "Cold State" and one labeled "Hot State".

    *   **Trends and Key Data Points:**
        *   **Cold State:** As the inner swirl blade angle *a* increases, the volume of the CTRZ generally increases.
        *   **Hot State:** In the hot state, the CTRZ volume is noticeably smaller compared to the cold state for corresponding angles. At low angles (a=30°), the combustion is deemed “unable to sustain”.
        *   The shape and location of the CTRZ change with varying angle values.
        *   The volume saturates from a = 39° onwards

    *   **Conclusions:** The inner swirl blade angle significantly influences the CTRZ morphology. Increasing the angle initially promotes the formation and expansion of the CTRZ. The heat release due to combustion reduces the CTRZ volume, as seen when comparing the “cold state” and “hot state”.

3.  **Tables:**

    There are no tables present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    The primary diagrams show the morphology of the Central Toroidal Recirculation Zone (CTRZ) in a combustor. The schematics are organized by different swirl angles and different temperature state.
    * The diagrams show the combustor’s geometry as a grey solid, with the CTRZ volumes shown as a translucent blue. 
    * CTRZ (or CRZ), which is the central recirculation zone is indicated with an arrow and text annotation.

In the cold state, positive tangential velocity grows with increasing dimensionless radial distance and reaches a peak at intermediate locations, then begins to decline. In the hot state, the same trend ensues. Values and gradient is much larger in the hot state than in the cold state.

        *   Axes: x-axis: Axial Distance (mm); y-axis: Axial Velocity (m/s).
        *   Trends: Axial velocity as a function of axial distance for different swirl angles at a fixed radial and azimuthal location (x=0, z=0mm). Axial velocity is always negative, but increases as axial distance increases. In hot state, negative axial velocity is significantly larger magnitude.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 12]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

There are no explicit mathematical equations written in formula notation on the page. The analysis relies on visual data and the interpretation of trends.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Description:** The figure (Fig. 14) shows contour plots representing the mass fraction of gas-phase kerosene (left column) and temperature (right column) within a combustion chamber. Each row corresponds to a different inner swirl blade angle (*a*), ranging from 33° to 51° in 3° increments.

*   **Axes:**
    *   *x*-axis: Represents the axial distance from the combustor inlet. The x-axis label is not explicitly stated in the figure but can be inferred by the scale: -40 to 60 on the left and -40 to 80 on the right
    *  *y*-axis: Represents the radial distance from the combustor centerline. The y-axis label is not explicitly stated in the figure but can be inferred by the scale: -20 to 20
    *   Color scale: Mass fraction of gas-phase kerosene and temperature, ranging from 0 to 1 and 300 K to 1900 K respectively, indicated by the color bar.

*   **Trends and Key Data:**
    *   *Gas-phase Kerosene*: As the swirl blade angle *a* increases, the penetration of the kerosene spray into the combustion chamber increases. The region of high kerosene mass fraction shifts forward from the inlet.
    *   *Temperature*: As the swirl blade angle *a* increases, the high-temperature region becomes more compact and concentrated. The location of the peak temperature moves downstream with increasing swirl.
    *   The contours reveal the presence of a recirculation zone, which influences the distribution of kerosene vapor and temperature.

*   **Conclusions:** The inner swirl blade angle significantly affects the fuel-air mixing and combustion process. Higher swirl angles lead to better fuel penetration and a more intense combustion zone.

**3. Tables:**

There are no tables on the page.

**4. Diagrams and Schematics:**

The figure shows a schematic representation of a generic combustion chamber with an inner swirl blade. The geometry is not fully detailed, but it indicates the presence of a swirler and a confined region for combustion. The representation shows the flow entering the combustion chamber.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 13]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   There are no explicit mathematical equations on the page. The velocity isosurfaces implicitly represent equations for velocity fields, but those are not written out.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 15: Effect of the inner swirl blade angles on emissions.**
        *   **X-axis:** Inner Swirl Blade Angles a (degrees)
        *   **Y-axis (left):** CO Emission (ppm @ 15% O2)
        *   **Y-axis (right):** NOx Emission (ppm @ 15% O2)
        *   **Trends:** The graph shows the effect of varying the inner swirl blade angle (a) on CO and NOx emissions.  CO emissions (blue squares) initially decrease with increasing swirl angle, then level out. The note "Unable to sustain combustion" exists near 32 degrees. NOx emissions (red squares) are relatively stable with increasing inner swirl vane angle - but showing a slight decrease overall.
        *   **Conclusions:** There's an inverse relationship between the inner swirl angle and CO emissions at lower angles.  NOx emissions are only slightly impacted by change to the inner swirl angle.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   There are no tables.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 16: Morphology of CTRZ at different outer swirl blade angles.**
        *   **Description:** This figure presents a series of 3D isosurfaces depicting the shape of the Corner Toroidal Recirculation Zone (CTRZ) and Central Recirculation Zone (CRZ) within a combustor. The isosurface corresponds to the location where Velocity-y = 0 m/s.
        *   **Organization:** The figure is organized in rows, with each row representing a different outer swirl blade angle (b), ranging from 35° to 60° in increments of 5°.  Within each row, two CTRZ representations are shown:
            *   **Cold State:** CTRZ morphology in a cold flow i.e. without combustion
            *   **Hot State:** CTRZ morphology in a hot state.
        *   **Trends:**  The images show the changes in size and shape of CTRZ as the outer blade swirl angle varies. The relative size, shape of the recirculation zones changes as swirl intensity changes.
        *   **Conclusions:**The CTRZ size is effected by outer swirl vane angle. Combustion (hot state) significantly changes the morphology relative to cold states.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 16]
1.  **Equations:**
    *   No mathematical equations are explicitly presented on this page.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 20: Effect of the outer swirl blade angles on emissions.**
        *   X-axis: Outer Swirl Blade Angles b (degrees) ranging from 35 to 60.
        *   Y-axis (left): CO Emission (ppm @ 15% O2) ranging from 3.2 to 5.2.
        *   Y-axis (right): NOx Emission (ppm @ 15% O2) ranging from 16 to 20.
        *   Trends: CO emission decreases as the outer swirl blade angle increases. NOx emission increases as the outer swirl blade angle increases.
        *   Optimal swirl angle range is identified to be between 40-50 degrees.
    *   **Figure 21: Morphology of CTRZ at different inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios.**
        *   Individual subplots for each inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio, Ra. Values of Ra are: 1:4, 1:3, 1:2, 2:3, 3:4, 1:1, 4:3, 3:2, 2:1, 3:1 and 4:1
        *   Each subplot shows an iso-surface (defined by velocity = 0 m/s) representing the CRTZ (central recirculation zone), in axial view.
        *  Axes: X and Y ranges both vary between -20 and +20
        *   The plots are presented for two states: "Cold State" and "Hot State." The "Hot State" condition for Ra = 3:4 shows "Unable to Sustain Combustion" instead of a CRTZ profile.
        *   Trends: Under cold conditions, as Ra increases, the size of CTRZ first decreases and then increases. When Ra is between 1:2 and 3:2, CTRZ is notably smaller. R = 3:4, 1:1, and 4:3 show no CTRZ. Under hot sate, Ra between 1:2 and 3:2 did not generate enough overall swirl intensity to maintain combustion. Deviation from 1:1 results in the swirl intensity becoming greater and combustion reaction significantly increases tangential velocity under the hot state.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   There are no tables shown on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**
    *   Figure 21 depicts a series of iso-surface plots illustrating the morphology of the CTRZ at different inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios (Ra). The iso-surface plots represent where the y-component of velocity is equal to 0 m/s. The plots are presented in two conditions: cold state and a hot state.  These plots visually represent the size and shape of the recirculation zone, which is critical for flame stabilization in combustion systems. The Ra values range from 1:4 to 4:1, representing variations in the ratio of air flow in the inner passage and outer passage. At Ra=3:4, the plot indicates the test has failed to sustain combustion.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 17]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

There are no explicit mathematical equations present on this page besides variable denotations.  However, the use of the term Ra indicates the ratio of the inner-to-outer air flow rates.  The text also refers to CTRZ volume (av) and recirculation rate (nq)

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

The page presents eight graphs, all related to velocity distributions under cold and hot flow conditions. All graphs depend on the "inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios Ra" as a parameter.  The graphs show variations in radial, axial, and tangential velocities with respect to either dimensionless radial distance or axial distance.

*   **(a) Radial Velocity vs. Dimensionless Radial Distance (Cold State):**
    *   X-axis: Dimensionless Radial Distance (0 to 1) at y=20mm
    *   Y-axis: Radial Velocity (m/s) (-0.6 to 0.6 approximate range)
    *   Ra parameter: 4:1, 3:2, 3:4, 3:1, 4:3, 2:3, 1:3, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:4
    *   Trends: The radial velocity profiles exhibit variations depending on Ra.  Some profiles show a local minimum, indicating flow reversal.  The magnitude of the radial velocity is generally higher near the outer edge.
*   **(b) Radial Velocity vs. Dimensionless Radial Distance (Hot State):**
    *   X-axis: Dimensionless Radial Distance (0 to 1) at y=20mm
    *   Y-axis: Radial Velocity (m/s) (-4 to 2 approximate range)
    *   Ra parameter: 4:1, 2:1, 1:4, 3:1, 1:3
    *   Trends: Similar to the cold state, the radial velocity changes with Ra. However, the hot state profiles generally show lower radial velocities.  The magnitude of negative (reversal) radial velocities is larger than positive ones.
*   **(c) Axial Velocity vs. Dimensionless Radial Distance (Cold State):**
    *   X-axis: Dimensionless Radial Distance (0 to 1) at y=20mm
    *   Y-axis: Axial Velocity (m/s) (-6 to 6 approximate range)
    *   Ra parameter: 4:1, 3:2, 3:4, 3:1, 4:3, 2:3, 1:3, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:4
    *   Trends: The axial velocity profiles show distinct variations based on Ra. A negative dip near the center, representing a CTRZ is observed. It becomes a local maximum near the outer edge.
*   **(d) Axial Velocity vs. Dimensionless Radial Distance (Hot State):**
    *   X-axis: Dimensionless Radial Distance (0 to 1) at y=20mm
    *   Y-axis: Axial Velocity (m/s) (-8 to 8 approximate range)
    *   Ra parameter: 4:1, 2:1, 1:4, 3:1, 1:3
    *   Trends: The axial velocity profiles are different compared to the cold state.  The CTRZ dip is attenuated. The profile generally shifts towards positive axial velocity, indicative of the effects of combustion.
*   **(e) Tangential Velocity vs. Dimensionless Radial Distance (Cold State):**
    *   X-axis: Dimensionless Radial

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 18]
1.  **Equations:**

    There are no explicit mathematical equations presented on this page. However, there are variables and ratios representing physical quantities related to combustion. Notably, "Ra" represents the Inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio.
     There are variables for swirl intensity "av" and "nq".

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    There is one graph, located at the top left of the image.
    *   **Axes:** The x-axis represents "Inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios (Ra)", with values ranging from 1:4 to 4:1.  The left y-axis represents "av", the overall swirl intensity of air flow, with a range between -0.1 and 0.6. The right y-axis represents "nq', with a range from-0.05 to 0.2.
    *   **Trends and Data:** The graph contains four curves with different markers.  Two curves represent the swirl intensity "av" in a "cold state" (cs) and "hot state" (hs), respectively. The other two correspond to 'nq' in a "cold state" and a "hot state". The 'hs' curve generally indicates a higher swirl intensity or value, in either state. The swirl trends vary depending on the airflow ratio, and the behavior changes significantly around Ra=2:1.
    *   **Conclusions:** Based on the text describing Figure 23, when a under cold state is below 0.2, combustion cannot happen.

3.  **Tables:**

    There are no explicitly defined tables.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    There are a series of contour plots on the lower half of the image.
    *   The figure displays the mass fraction of gas-phase kerosene and temperature contours at different inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios.
    *   There are horizontal rows, with each representing a different inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio "Ra" (1:4, 1:3, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1).
    *   Each row contains two contours: "Mass Fraction of Gas-phase Kerosene", "Temperature".
    *   **Mass Fraction of Kerosene:** The kerosene concentration contours generally show a 'V' shape distribution in the combustion chamber. The concentration and shape vary with "Ra".
    *   **Temperature Contours:** The temperature contours display the distribution of high-temperature zones within the combustion chamber. The temperature distribution also varies with "Ra", with the hightest temperature appearing as a 'hot spot' at the center of the combustor. A color bar ranges from 300K to 1900K illustrating the temperature magnitude.
    *The x and y axis of the schematics represents distance in unspecified units, ranging between -40 to 80 on the X axis and -20 to 20 on the Y axis.
    *   **Conclusions:** These contours demonstrate the complex interaction between airflow ratios, fuel distribution, and temperature profiles in a combustion chamber. The 'Ra' value significantly influences the kerosene distribution and the location/intensity of the high-temperature zones, affecting combustion efficiency and emissions.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 19]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

*   Equation (27): a_v-hs = 0.027 + 0.05 * a_v-cs
*   Equation (28): eta_q-hs = -0.017 + 0.969 * eta_q-cs

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 25:** This graph plots CO Emission (ppm @15% O2) and NOx Emission (ppm @ 15% O2) on the y-axes against Inner-to-Outer Air Flow Rate Ratio (R_a) on the x-axis.
    *   CO emission generally decreases as the inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio increases past 1:4.
    *   NOx emission tends to increase as the inner-to-outer air flow rate ratio increases up to 3:2, and possibly decreases slightly afterwards.
    *   A region labeled "Unable to sustain combustion" exists at low inner-to-outer air flow rate ratios (1:4 to approximately 2:3).
*   **Figure 26:** This graph plots the relationship between the swirl intensity parameter "a_v" under cold state (a_v-cs) on the x-axis and the swirl intensity parameter "a_v" under hot state (a_v-hs) on the y-axis.
    *   A linear regression of the data is shown: y = 0.027 + 0.05 * x, with an R^2 value of 0.62.  This indicates a moderate linear positive correlation.
    *   The a_v-cs values range from 0.2 to 0.39.
*   **Figure 27:** This graph plots the relationship between the swirl intensity parameter "eta_q" under cold state (eta_q-cs) on the x-axis and the swirl intensity parameter "eta_q" under hot state (eta_q-hs) on the y-axis.
    *   A linear regression of the data is shown: y = -0.017 + 0.969 * x, with an R^2 value of 0.69. This indicates a relatively strong linear positive correlation.
    *   The eta_q-cs values range from 0.131 to 0.165.
*   **Figure 28:** This graph shows the boundary of stable combustion, plotted as eta_q-cs on the y-axis versus a_v-cs on the x-axis. A region of "Unable to sustain combustion" exists when both eta_q-cs < 0.125 and a_v-cs < 0.2. A region of "Able to sustain combustion" exists when both eta_q-cs > 0.125 and a_v-cs > 0.2. The points are further classified as "Able combustion" or "Unstable combustion".

**3. Tables:**

There are no tables on this page.

**4. Diagrams or Schematics:**

There are no diagrams or schematics present on this page.

title: 1-s2.0-S1359431125039031-main
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 20]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   eta_cs = 0.02 + 0.387 * a_v_cs
    *   eta_hs = 0.012 + 2.67 * a_v_hs

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 29:**
        *   **Axes:**
            *   x-axis: "a_v" (dimensionless CTRZ volume)
            *   y-axis: "eta" (recirculation rate)
        *   **Trends:** Two sets of data are plotted on the graph; "Cold State" and "Hot State"
        *   **Cold State Data:** A mostly linear upward trend with a lower slope. Data range for a_v is 0.03 to 0.17.
            *   Regression fit: y = 0.02+0.389x with R^2=0.903
        *   **Hot State Data:** An upward linear trend with a steeper slope. Data range for a_v is 0 to 0.7.
            *   Regression fit: y = 0.012+2.67x with R^2=0.969
        *   **Key Data Points:**  The graph shows values are clustered at the lower part of the x-axis.
        *   **Conclusions:** The hot state shows a higher relationship with a_v than the cold state when considering the recirculation rate. This implies that an increase in both CTRZ volume correlates to increased mixing and burning. This trend can be described by the linear relationship and R^2 values. The analysis of the fitting results indicates a distinct linear relationship between a_v and eta. The slope is much higher under hot burning conditions.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   There are no tables present on the page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   There are no diagrams or schematics present on the page.
